blocks.text: Jason Vasquez
General Manager
{{fact:f:E-2018-001.client}}
Re: Retained Search, Head of Operations
Dear Jason,

Thank you for asking us to find your next Head of Operations. This letter sets out what we will do, who will do it, and what it costs, and once you sign it below it is the agreement between {{fact:f:E-2018-001.client}} and Northgate Talent Partners LLC. I have kept it short. If anything in it does not match what we agreed on the phone, tell me before you sign it rather than after.
Scope of the Search

The assignment is a retained search for a Head of Operations at Davis and Sons, reporting to you. We will not approach the market until you and I have agreed a written position specification. That document will state the mandate, the first-year priorities, the compensation range you are prepared to pay, and the two or three things that would rule out an otherwise strong candidate. Drafting it takes about two weeks of back and forth and it is worth every day of it. Most searches that go wrong go wrong here, because the client and the consultant were describing different jobs to each other and neither of them noticed until a finalist turned up in the room.

The search is exclusive to the firm for its term. You are engaging us, not a listing service, and the retainer buys a systematic canvass of the market rather than whoever happens to be looking this month. We will approach people who are employed and content, which is most of the people worth having, and that is slow work by design. Expect a considered slate of finalists rather than a stream of resumes. If you have internal candidates or people you have already met, tell us now and we will assess them alongside the market at no additional fee. It is far cheaper to include them at the start than to discover them late.
Approach
Our method is unremarkable and we run it the same way every time:

items: Position specification, agreed with you and signed off before any outreach begins.
Market mapping: a written universe of the companies and functions where this person is likely to sit today.
Approach and screening: confidential conversations conducted by our consultants, not by a researcher reading from a script.
Slate presentation: written candidate summaries and our own assessment, delivered in person.
Client interviews, referencing, and support through offer, acceptance, and resignation.

blocks.text: Two points on that list matter more than the rest. First, we describe candidates to you by their current role and responsibilities, and their names do not go into anything that circulates until they have told us they may. Discretion is the whole business. A candidate exposed to their employer because of us is a candidate we will never place again, and word travels faster in this market than any of us would like. Second, we present a slate rather than a single name, and we present it in person, so that you can argue with us while we are in the room to answer.
Your Team

I will lead the engagement, and you will see me at the specification meeting and at the slate presentation. James Grant will run it day to day and he is the person you should call. Jim has been with the firm since the start and knows this market better than I do; he also has the useful habit of telephoning rather than writing, so do not be surprised to get a call about something I would have put in a note. Nicole Donovan will do the research and build the market map. She is the reason our candidate descriptions are accurate about scope and title, which sounds like a small thing until you have watched a search fall apart because someone's job had been described a level higher than it actually sat.
Fee and Commencement

The fee for this assignment is {{fact:f:E-2018-001.fee}}, fixed for the term of the search regardless of the compensation the successful candidate ultimately negotiates. We do not work on contingency and we do not take a percentage of the hire's package, because that arrangement puts our interest and yours on opposite sides of the table at the exact moment you are trying to close. The engagement commences on {{fact:f:E-2018-001.start}}, and we plan on roughly five months from commencement to a signed acceptance, assuming your interview slots are available when we need them. That last condition is not boilerplate. A slow client calendar is the most common reason a good candidate goes somewhere else.
Fees and Payment

The fee is invoiced in three installments: at commencement, on presentation of the slate, and on acceptance of an offer. Invoices are payable within thirty days of receipt. Reasonable out-of-pocket expenses, meaning travel to interviews and candidate travel where you ask us to arrange it, are billed at cost against receipts. We will tell you before we incur anything out of the ordinary.
Term and Termination

The engagement runs from commencement until the position is filled or until either of us ends it. Either party may terminate on written notice. If you terminate, you owe the installments already invoiced and nothing beyond them. If we terminate, which we would do only if the assignment turns out to be one we cannot honestly complete, we will refund any installment covering work not performed. For the term of this engagement and for one year after it ends, we will not recruit from your company.
Confidentiality

What you tell us about the business, the role, and the reason it is open stays inside the engagement team. The same protection runs the other way: what a candidate tells us in confidence is theirs, and we will not repeat it to you unless they permit it. Neither party will use the other's name in marketing without written consent, which means Davis and Sons does not appear on our client list unless you tell us it may.
Limitation of Liability

We will conduct the search with the care and skill you are entitled to expect from us. We do not guarantee an outcome and we cannot warrant the future performance of a person you choose to hire; that decision is yours and the risk in it stays with you. Our aggregate liability arising out of this engagement is limited to the fees actually paid to us under it, and neither party is liable to the other for indirect or consequential loss.
Governing Law

This letter and any dispute arising out of it are governed by the laws of the state in which our principal office is located, without regard to its conflict of laws provisions. It is the entire agreement between us on this assignment and it supersedes anything either of us said before it.

If this reflects our understanding, please sign both copies and return one to me; the second is yours to keep. Jim will call to schedule the specification meeting as soon as the signed copy is back, and I would rather he did not have to wait on it. If you want anything in here changed, call me directly and we will change it or explain why we cannot.
Sincerely,
To: Nicole Donovan
From: James Grant
Re: Kickoff, Head of Operations search for {{fact:f:E-2018-001.client}}

Nicole, following on from the call this morning, and putting it in writing because Kelly will ask me whether I did. The letter is signed, the engagement commenced on {{fact:f:E-2018-001.start}}, and we are on the clock. The objective is one hire: a Head of Operations for {{fact:f:E-2018-001.client}}, reporting to Jason Vasquez, with a signed acceptance inside five months. Everything below hangs off that single sentence, and if a piece of work does not serve it, we are not doing it. Nothing has been said to the market yet and I want to keep it that way until the specification is agreed. The order of operations is not negotiable here; it is what I told Jason on the phone and it is what Kelly wrote into the letter.
Workstreams

items: Position specification. I draft it, you check the scope and the titles, Kelly reads it before it goes over. I want sign-off in writing rather than on a call. The last time we took a verbal on a spec we rewrote the thing twice and the client remembered it differently than we did both times.

Market map. Yours. The universe of companies where this person sits today, by segment, with the function and the likely title inside each one. Written down, not in your head. I want to be able to argue with it.
Approach and screening. Mine, with you on the first pass of the obvious ones. No scripts. If someone is worth calling, they are worth me calling.
Client management. Also mine. Jason is straightforward to deal with, but he goes quiet when things are busy at his end, so I will keep the cadence rather than wait on him to surface.
Documentation. You own the file. Every conversation logged the same day it happens, and candidates described by their current role in anything that leaves this office.

blocks.text: Next Steps

items: Specification meeting on site at Davis and Sons. I will get an hour with Jason and with whoever this person will actually work alongside. Come to that one. You hear things in those rooms that I miss because I am talking.
First cut of the map two weeks from the specification meeting, not two weeks from today. The clock on your piece starts when we know what we are looking for.

Compensation. We need a range we believe before we call anyone. Jason gave Kelly a number over the phone and I am not confident it survives contact with his board, so I want it confirmed in the specification and not in an email.
File and client folder set up today if you can manage it.
Reference plan. Not yet, but think about it now. By the time we need references we will need them fast, and I do not want to be inventing the approach while a finalist waits.

blocks.text: On the client side, a word about how this one is likely to run. Jason Vasquez is the decision maker and he says so, which is more than you get most places, but the people around him will have opinions and some of those opinions will arrive late. That is normal and it is survivable as long as we surface them at the specification stage rather than at the slate. So in that meeting, listen for who talks about the job as though they have already imagined a particular person in it. That is the one who derails a finalist in week fourteen, and I would rather know their name now.

One other thing, and it matters more than the list does. The person we place here is somebody's number two somewhere right now, two years into a job, and not unhappy. That means the first call matters more than the tenth, because by the tenth we are calling people because they are on a list rather than because they are right. Do not let me get there. If the map says someone, tell me why in one sentence. If you cannot do it in one sentence, they are not on the map.
Jim
Jason, Nicole,

Writing rather than calling, since you said you are in meetings all week and I would rather you had this in front of you. The specification for {{fact:f:E-2018-001.client}} is signed, the map is at first draft, and the universe is larger than I expected. It will come down hard once Nicole has cut the companies where the operations function is too small to have produced the person you want.

Approaches started this week. There is nothing to show you yet, and I would not want to show you anything yet; at this stage a name is only a name. A couple of the conversations have gone well enough that I would call them live, both people sitting in the number two seat where they are, which is exactly what we said we were looking for.

Two asks. First, the compensation range in the spec is the one you gave Kelly. I need to know it holds at the top end if the right person is at the top end, and I would rather you tested that now than when we have a finalist in hand. Second, block interview time well before we need it and have Nicole hold the days. We lose more people to a client who took three weeks to find an hour than to anything else in this process.

For the file, this thread now stands as the record of what Jason Vasquez asked for and what Nicole Donovan and I committed to in return, which saves all three of us a memo. Copy Nicole on anything to do with scheduling for Davis and Sons so it does not sit in my inbox while I am on the road.
James Grant

Working session on the Head of Operations search for {{fact:f:E-2018-001.client}}, held at the client's offices. The purpose was to review the state of the approach list and to agree what the client will see when the slate is presented.
Attendees

items: James Grant, Senior Consultant
Nicole Donovan, Research Associate
Jason Vasquez, General Manager, Davis and Sons

blocks.text: Discussion

Mr. Grant opened by restating the mandate: one hire, a Head of Operations reporting to Mr. Vasquez, on a search running roughly five months end to end. He noted that we are near the middle of that span. No candidate is identified by name in this record, in keeping with the firm's standing practice on a live search.

The market map is complete and has been cut twice against the signed specification. The working universe now covers the segments agreed at kickoff, and the approach list drawn from it is at a size we can actually work rather than a size that flatters the research. Mr. Grant has had first conversations across most of that list. A handful he characterized as live. Two he described as being polite, and I share that read on both; neither has asked a question about the role that a genuinely interested person would have asked by now.

The live candidates were reviewed in current-role terms. They comprise a deputy running operations at a substantially larger business, a plant-level general manager with multi-site responsibility, and a supply chain director whose remit is narrower than the mandate but who is, on Mr. Grant's assessment, the strongest operator of the group. Mr. Vasquez asked whether the last of these should be discounted for scope. Mr. Grant's position was that scope can be added to a person and judgment cannot, and that he would make the argument properly at the slate rather than settle it in this session.

Mr. Vasquez confirmed that the compensation range in the specification holds at its upper end for the right candidate, and said he would rather have that tested now than after someone has been through a full interview cycle. He asked that the slate be presented in person rather than sent ahead, which matches our practice and was already the plan. Scheduling was raised again. Mr. Vasquez will hold interview time and confirm the specific days to me in writing. Mr. Grant repeated his view that a slow calendar loses more candidates than a weak offer does, and Mr. Vasquez did not disagree with him.

One process note, recorded at Mr. Grant's request. Two people on the approach list have asked that nothing identifying them go in writing to Davis and Sons before they have decided whether to enter the process. That is our practice regardless of the request, but it was worth the client hearing it said aloud: the summaries at the slate will describe candidates by their current responsibilities, and names follow once each candidate has released them to us.
Action Items

rows: Complete the remaining first approaches on the working list
Draft written candidate summaries in current-role form
Confirm blocked interview days in writing
Confirm the upper end of the compensation range with the board
Log this session's read on candidate interest to the engagement file

blocks.text: Next working session to be scheduled at the client's convenience. Minutes recorded by Nicole Donovan.
Status Summary

The search for a Head of Operations at Davis and Sons is on schedule at the midpoint. The position specification is signed, the market map is complete, and first approaches are done across the working list. We are assembling the slate now and will present it in person, with the written summaries handed over in the room rather than sent ahead of it. No offer has been made and none should be expected at this stage. This is the stretch of a search where nothing visible happens for a couple of weeks and then all of it happens at once.
This Period

items: Position specification agreed with {{fact:f:E-2018-001.client}} and signed off before any outreach.
Market map completed by Nicole Donovan and cut twice against the specification.
First approaches completed across the working list, conducted by me rather than by a researcher.
Working session held with your team to review the approach list and agree the shape of the slate.
Written candidate summaries drafted in current-role form, names withheld pending each candidate's release.

blocks.text: The useful news from this period is not the number of conversations, it is the read underneath them. There is a real market for this role. The people who fit it are employed, they were not looking when we found them, and every one of them had to be talked into a first conversation. That is what a retained assignment buys, and it is worth saying plainly at the halfway mark: a contingency shop would have put resumes on your desk inside a week and none of them would have been these people.

A word on what the map bought us, since you are paying for it. Nicole Donovan built the universe from the specification rather than from a database, which meant a slower start and a much shorter list at the end. The companies on it were chosen because their operations function is large enough and complex enough to have produced somebody who can do your job, and the ones that fell out fell out for stated reasons that are written down. If you want to see the reasoning, ask her at the slate session. She will show you, and she will be right.
Engagement Budget

Nothing has changed on the commercial side. The engagement fee stands at {{fact:f:E-2018-001.fee}} as set out in the engagement letter, fixed for the term and not indexed to whatever the successful candidate ends up negotiating. The first installment has been invoiced. The second falls due on presentation of the slate and the balance on acceptance of an offer. Out-of-pocket expenses to date are modest and consist of travel to your offices and to candidate meetings. Nothing out of the ordinary has been incurred and nothing is anticipated. If that changes, you will hear it from me before it reaches an invoice rather than on one.
Upcoming Work

items: Complete the referencing groundwork on the likely finalists, without approaching any current employer.
Present the slate in person, with Nicole Donovan attending to answer on scope and background.
Support your interview cycle: brief each candidate before they see you and debrief them afterward, and tell you what they actually said rather than what they told you.
Manage the offer and resignation stage if and when we get there.

blocks.text: Risks

Calendar. This is the real one and I am not going to dress it up. The strongest people on our list are being courted elsewhere, which is what strong people are always doing, and the interview time you hold over the next few weeks is what decides whether they stay in our process or someone else's. I would rather ask you for time now and hand it back unused than go looking for it later.

Compensation. The range in the specification is the range you gave us, and the market is testing its upper end rather than its middle. That is not a surprise and it is not yet a problem. It becomes a problem if the number needs a conversation with your board at the exact moment we have a finalist waiting on an answer.

Scope. One candidate we rate highly sits a notch below the mandate on breadth. If your team screens on the organization chart rather than on judgment, that conversation ends early and I believe it would be the wrong call. We will make the argument once, in the slate session, and after that it is your decision and we will back it.
Confidentiality. Unchanged and holding. Nobody on the list has been exposed, and nothing identifying any of them has gone in writing to Davis and Sons.

None of the four is a reason to change course, and I would tell you if one were. A search at the midpoint should have exactly this shape: the work is done, the people are found, and everything that remains is timing and nerve.
As always, call rather than write if any of this needs a conversation. Nicole Donovan is copied and has the file if I am on the road.
Prepared by James Grant, Senior Consultant

Closing working session on the Head of Operations search for {{fact:f:E-2018-001.client}}, held by telephone. The purpose was to confirm the position is filled, to record what remains outstanding, and to agree the close-out of the engagement file.
Attendees

items: James Grant, Senior Consultant
Nicole Donovan, Research Associate
Jason Vasquez, General Manager, Davis and Sons

blocks.text: Discussion

Mr. Grant confirmed that the offer has been accepted and that the candidate has resigned his current position. The accepted candidate is the plant-level general manager with multi-site responsibility who was presented on the original slate. He was not the candidate the client favored after first interview, a point Mr. Vasquez raised himself and said he was glad to have been argued with about. Notice period and start arrangements sit between the candidate and Davis and Sons and are not recorded here.

A short discussion followed on the finalist who withdrew. The withdrawal came late in the process and Mr. Grant handled it by telephone rather than by note, which Mr. Vasquez said he appreciated hearing. The stated reason was a counter-offer. My own read, recorded for the file, is that the counter-offer was the occasion rather than the cause, and that the length of the interview cycle did more damage than any of us wanted to say at the time. Mr. Grant did not disagree. It is worth carrying into the next assignment with this client.

Mr. Vasquez asked about our position after the placement. Mr. Grant restated what the engagement letter says: the fee is fixed and its balance falls due on acceptance, and the firm will not recruit from Davis and Sons for the term of this engagement and for a year after it ends. Mr. Vasquez then asked whether we would take a second search. Mr. Grant said that was a conversation for Kelly and that he would arrange it rather than answer for her.

Mr. Grant then asked me to record something for the file that he said he would otherwise forget to say. The placement came off a slate rather than off a single introduction, and it came off the part of the slate the client had not expected to hire from. He credited the map for that: the accepted candidate sat in a segment the client had not named at kickoff, and he was on the list because the research put him there and not because anybody in this firm already knew him. I record it as he said it. He also asked that the client be told plainly that the search ran to its planned length and did not run over, which Mr. Vasquez acknowledged.

On close-out, I will hold the candidate file for the period our retention practice requires and dispose of it after that. The market map stays with the firm. Nothing identifying an unsuccessful candidate goes to the client, now or later, and Mr. Vasquez confirmed he had never asked for any of it.
Action Items

rows: Issue the final invoice on acceptance, per the engagement letter
Confirm the candidate's start arrangements directly with the client
Write to the candidates who were not selected
Close the engagement file and archive the market map
Arrange a conversation with the client on further work

blocks.text: No further session scheduled. Minutes recorded by Nicole Donovan.
